description:
You are a capacity-planning engineer who needs to set up secure, key-based access to a fleet of machines before you can begin collecting utilisation statistics.  
Perform the following steps in a Bash shell (you do not have root privileges):

1. Generate a brand-new **4096-bit RSA** SSH keypair whose *base name* is  
   /home/user/.ssh/capacity_planner  
   • The private key must therefore be exactly  
     /home/user/.ssh/capacity_planner  
   • The public key must therefore be exactly  
     /home/user/.ssh/capacity_planner.pub  

2. Immediately verify the key by printing its SHA256 fingerprint in “visual” format (the format produced by `ssh-keygen -lf …`).  
   • You may not add a pass-phrase to the key.  
   • Do **not** display the private key itself.

3. Create a single plain-text log file named  
   /home/user/capacity_planner_report.log  
   with the **precise** four-section layout shown below.  Populate the dynamic fields as instructed:  

   ----------------------------------------------------------------------
   Line 1  ISO-8601 timestamp  (UTC)  followed by one blank space, then
           the short hostname of the current machine (output of `hostname -s`)
           Example pattern that the grader will look for:  
           2026-03-14T15:09:26Z myhost

   Line 2  An empty line (must be literally blank).

   Line 3  The literal header text (exact spelling & capitals):  
           === PUBLIC KEY FINGERPRINT ===

   Line 4  The full fingerprint string exactly as produced in step 2.  
           It must start with “SHA256:” and end with the key size, e.g.  
           SHA256:f1nGeRpRiNtFoObAr 4096
   ----------------------------------------------------------------------

4. Correct permissions:  
   • /home/user/.ssh/capacity_planner           – 0600 (rw——)  
   • /home/user/.ssh/capacity_planner.pub       – 0644 (rw-r-r-)  
   • /home/user/capacity_planner_report.log     – 0644 (rw-r-r-)

After finishing, leave the terminal ready for the next command prompt; no extra output is required.

The automated checker will confirm **all** of the following:
• Both key files exist at the exact paths given and have the required permissions.  
• The public key’s first field literally begins with “ssh-rsa”.  
• /home/user/capacity_planner_report.log exists, contains exactly four non-empty logical lines structured as described, and line 4 begins with “SHA256:” and ends with “4096”.  
• The timestamp on line 1 is valid ISO-8601 UTC (ends with “Z”).  
If any test fails, the task will be marked incorrect.
truth:
The judge will verify:

1. File paths & perms  
   /home/user/.ssh/capacity_planner            mode 100600  
   /home/user/.ssh/capacity_planner.pub        mode 100644  
   /home/user/capacity_planner_report.log      mode 100644  

2. Public key content  
   – First token exactly "ssh-rsa".  
   – At least 600 characters total to ensure 4096-bit size (approx).

3. Log file regexes  
   Line 1: ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z[ ]+[A-Za-z0-9._-]+$  
   Line 2: ^$  
   Line 3: ^=== PUBLIC KEY FINGERPRINT ===$  
   Line 4: ^SHA256:[A-Za-z0-9+/=]{43,}[ ]+4096$  

4. The fingerprint string on line 4 must match the output of  
   ssh-keygen -lf /home/user/.ssh/capacity_planner | awk '{print $2" "$1}'  
   (ssh-keygen prints “4096 SHA256:xyz… filename”, so we transpose fields.)

There is no pre-existing key or report file before the task starts.
